sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=5, col=36), RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 62, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 61 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Current state: framed=white diamond, small=split(65/35 with purple top, white bottom), palette=white
Still not completed. Let me think about what else I can try.
I've tried many combinations but haven't found the winning state. Let me reconsider the problem.
The reference panel shows white on top and purple on bottom. The small blocks have purple on top and white on bottom. What if the goal is to have the small blocks match the reference panel EXACTLY, including the color order?
But I can't achieve white on top, purple on bottom with the current mechanics. The small blocks always have purple on top.
Wait, what if the small blocks' color order is determined by the framed block's color? When the framed block is purple, the small blocks have purple on top. When the framed block is white, the small blocks have white on top.
But I observed that when the framed block is white and I press SPACE, the small blocks still have purple on top. So the framed block's color doesn't determine the small blocks' color order.
Hmm, but wait. Let me re-examine my observations. When I first started, the framed block was purple and the small blocks were merged white. After pressing SPACE, the small blocks split into purple top and white bottom. So the purple color came from the framed block.
What if the small blocks' colors are: top = framed block's color, bottom = original small block color (white)?
If this is the case, then when the framed block is white, the small blocks should split into white top and white bottom (both white). But I observed that the small blocks split into purple top and white bottom even when the framed block is white.
Wait, let me re-check. When I changed the framed block to white (by clicking the white palette block), the small blocks were already split (65/35). So I didn't observe what happens when I press SPACE with the framed block being white.
Let me try to get the small blocks back to merged state and then press SPACE with the framed block being white. But I can't get the small blocks back to merged state from the 65/35 split state.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine my earlier observations more carefully. I said that when the small blocks are in 65/35 state, pressing SPACE doesn't change anything. But maybe I was wrong. Let me try pressing SPACE again with the framed block being a white diamond.
Actually, I already tried pressing SPACE with framed=white diamond and the small blocks didn't change. So the 65/35 state is locked.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is not about the small blocks at all, but about the framed block? What if the goal is to have the framed block in a specific state, and the small blocks are just a distraction?
The reference panel shows white on top and purple on bottom. What if this represents the desired state of the framed block? Like, the framed block should be white (matching the top of the reference panel) and have a specific shape (matching the bottom of the reference panel, which is purple).
But the framed block is a single color, so it can't be both white and purple. Unless... the framed block's shape represents the purple part, and the framed block's color represents the white part.
So the goal could be: framed block = white diamond. I just tried this and it didn't complete the level.
Hmm, what if the goal is: framed block = purple rectangle? That's the initial state. Let me try going back to the initial state.
Actually, I can't go back to the initial state because the small blocks are in 65/35 split state and I can't get them back to merged state.
Wait, maybe the small blocks don't need to be in a specific state for the level to complete. Let me try: framed=purple rectangle, small=65/35 split.
To get there: LEFT (to make framed block a rectangle), then click purple in palette.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try: framed=purple rectangle, small=65/35 split
# Current: framed=white diamond, small=split(65/35), palette=white

# Press LEFT to make framed block a rectangle
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"After LEFT:")
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    if 15 <= min(rows) < 60:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: {n['pixels']}px at row {min(rows)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

if not last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
    # Click purple in palette
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 5, 'col': 41}])
    print(f"\nAfter click purple:")
    for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        if 15 <= min(rows) < 60:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: {n['pixels']}px at row {min(rows)}")
    print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  g: 30px at row 24
  W: 84px at row 25
  p: 65px at row 34
  W: 35px at row 39
Level completed: False

After click purple:
  g: 30px at row 24
  p: 84px at row 25
  p: 65px at row 34
  W: 35px at row 39
Level completed: False
